summary: Gruber reviews the Kindle Paperwhite as an upgrade from the Kindle Keyboard, finding the new front-lit e-ink display preserving the peaceful feel of e-ink despite uneven lighting at the bottom. He criticizes the removal of dedicated page-turning buttons as a missed opportunity for a single-purpose reading device, arguing that touchscreen-only input requires unnecessary thumb movement. The review's sharpest critique targets Amazon's typography: poorly chosen fonts that don't suit e-ink rendering, and the inexcusable lack of hyphenation combined with full-justified text. Gruber argues that as a dedicated reading device, the Kindle has no excuse for mediocre typographic layout and font rendering, comparing it to audio fidelity expectations for an iPod. He concludes Amazon should hire a world-class book designer as product manager.
key_points: The Paperwhite's front lighting preserves e-ink's calming quality but suffers from uneven illumination at the bottom, giving it a low-end feel
Removing dedicated page-turning buttons was a mistake — a single-purpose reading device should optimize its core interaction with great hardware buttons, not force touchscreen-only input
The new font choices are poor: Futura is wrong for body text, Baskerville's thin strokes expose the display's resolution limits, and most readers will stick with the slab serif Caecilia by default
Full text justification without hyphenation is a typographic sin that a dedicated e-reader in 2012 has no excuse for committing
Amazon gets a pass on typography only because competitors are equally bad — their goal should be matching print typography quality
money_quotes: Glowing displays wind me up. Ink on paper and e-ink wind me down.
E-ink Kindles are the anti-iPad. Single-purpose vs. all-purpose.
To remain relevant in an iPad (and Kindle Fire) world, a single-purpose device like the Kindle Paperwhite needs an obsessive focus on the reading experience.
The old Kindles had way too many buttons. But one button, the power toggle, is too few.
Rather than showing off how nice the display is, Baskerville does the opposite: it shows how relatively crude it is, resolution-wise.
Full justification without hyphenation inevitably results in unsightly gaps between words on a few lines each page.
Typographic layout and font rendering are the Kindle's equivalents to audio fidelity from an iPod — there's no excuse for it to be less than good, and it ought to be downright excellent.
key_insight: A dedicated single-purpose reading device has no excuse for mediocre typography and interaction design — the Kindle Paperwhite's removal of page-turning buttons and its poor font choices undermine the very reading experience that justifies its existence alongside tablets.
